To date, PDP performance is ambiguous, at best. Though helpful, to some extent, to ﬁll the gap left in the color ratings data, the efﬂuent data on the limited number of ﬁrms are insufﬁcient to determine whether the regulatory disclosure has made a substantial contribution to improving participating ﬁrms’ envi- ronmental performance. To complement our quantitative analysis, we directly asked the ﬁrm managers for their view of the disclosure program. When asked, “What motivated you to improve, if ever, your environmental performance?”none of our respondents cited information disclosure as a motivator. No respondent reported facing external pressures (e.g. pressures from capital markets or surrounding communities) and enhanced internal learning, which are supposedly generated by disclosure of the performance ratings. The majority of ﬁrms interviewed point to traditional efﬂuent standards as their primary motivation, as compliance (i.e. a blue rating) is a sufﬁcient condition for the issuance of a Discharge Permit from LLDA. As explained previously, businesses under the LLDA’s jurisdiction cannot operate without either a Discharge Permit or certiﬁcate of exemption. The ﬁrms have responded to signals for formal sanctions and their position is indistinguishable from all other non-participating ﬁrms that need a Discharge Permit and are under corresponding obligation to ﬁle regular Self-Monitoring Reports (SMRs). It is, therefore, difﬁcult to separate the effect of regulatory disclosure from that of the traditional command-and-control requirements in explaining the rise in compliance during the ﬁrst three years of the program. In sum, we failed to ﬁnd evidence indicating that PDP has improved water quality or generated the theoretically anticipated disclosure effects. In what follows, we discuss why the potential forces of information disclosure were not realized. 4.2. What is going on with regulatory disclosure in the Philippines? Given that PDP has been unsuccessful at holding its promise of informational effects, it should be useful to parse out the reasons for the unsatisfactory outcomes to inform similar regulatory efforts elsewhere. From analysis of the programs’ record to date and consideration of the Philippines’ socio-political context, along with the interview data, we identify ﬂawed execution of disclosure systems and the limited administrative capacity of LLDA as impediments to effective implementation. 4.2.1. Flawed execution and design of disclosure systems One outstanding problem of PDP lies in a mismatch between a form of disclosed information and its target audience; that is, the information tailored for users (the public) was delivered mainly to disclosers (regulated ﬁrms). The conception of PDP’s color- coded rating was premised on turning complex technical information into a more user- friendly format. Admittedly, tailored information is useful to invoke legitimate public pressure because it is far more comprehensible in the eyes of the lay public. Yet, despite continuing to assign ratings to participating ﬁrms, the agency only discloses the relevant information directly to regulatees, not the public. As observed in Lee’s (2010) research on the Indonesian regulatory disclosure program, it is theoretically possible that information disclosure skewed toward regulated ﬁrms could still meet the intended policy goal if it could provide the ﬁrms with salient environmental parameters helping to enhance opportunities for internal learning to Regulation-by-information E. Lee et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 10 Page 11: correct their organizational failure. In the PDP case, however, the current practice pro- vides little value to participating ﬁrms, as the ratings simply reﬂect the pollution data earlier provided by the ﬁrms themselves to the agency under the standard Self- Monitoring Report (SMR) system imposed on all ﬁrms. The above argument is supported by comments from the ﬁrm managers indicating that there is no meaningful difference between requirements from PDP and SMR, which was initially designed as part of the command-and-control regulation. Under the SMR system, compliance (equivalent to a blue rating under PDP) merely means the renewal of a discharge permit. Although PDP participants are encouraged to reduce emissions at least 20 percent below permissible levels in order to be recognized as moving beyond compliance, PDP does not offer ﬁrms any technical instruction or impose additional reporting requirements in order to achieve their goals. As such, the current regulatory disclosure adds little to the existing command-and-control framework. In sum, the information provided by the agency is useful to neither intended users nor disclosers. An additional problem is with the procedural design of the program. As it stands, interaction between governmental and non-governmental actors is limited to that between the implementing agency and regulated ﬁrms. At no point are environmental NGOs involved in data veriﬁcation, which prevents public scrutiny of the regulatory process. This one-sided nature of regulatory interactions leads to an ersatz clientelist mode of governance wherein public interest groups suspect that the color-coded ratings may be the results of agency capture, rather than the impartial evaluation of the ﬁrms’ performance. Because the intended recipients of information do not have conﬁdence in the ratings, the information is discarded as unreliable. Admittedly, one way to address this credibility problem is to involve public interest groups in the data veriﬁcation process. A more straightforward way is to make disclosure automatic, seamless, and transparent – for example, having the government laboratories’ water quality measurements (of ﬁrm BOD and total suspended solids) automatically published on a website. As an illustration, SPM once took advantage of the performance ratings provided by the agency in selecting the Lason awardees and presented a list of the awardees to the public. This was an unusual instance, given that SPM did not trust the ﬁdelity of the ratings. What is noteworthy is that SPM used them on that occasion because they were provided with raw data that could justify the ratings. We do not mean to discount an advantage of comprehensible color-coded ratings over complicated numerical data. Our point is that, together with tailored information, raw data should also be publicly available, so that any interested parties (e.g. environ- mental groups, university researchers) can test the credibility of color-coded ratings and the general public can be convinced of the goodwill of a given regulation. At this point, we are cautious not to overlook the fact that full disclosure would be a radical step for Philippine state agencies. Here, the question of institutional ﬁt is that of the agency’s capacity to cope with a mode of action that is alien to it. 4.2.2. Institutional considerations Implementation success depends largely on the compatibility of policy designs with their institutional–organizational milieu (Lejano 2006; Lejano & Shankar 2012). Therefore, we ﬁrst consider essential features of political and administrative life in the Philippines, with a focus on the role of civil society in public policymaking and implementation. This consideration will serve as a basis for understanding the root of the implementation Regulation-by-information E. Lee et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 11 Page 12: challenges for public disclosure programs, associated with the characteristics of the country’s regulatory institutions. 4.3. The Philippine socio-political context Political life in the Philippines is a complex marriage of democratization and elitist capture. These elements are sometimes in conﬂict, but always somehow sustained in dynamic tension. The ﬂowering of civil society had its roots in the protests of the 1970s and 80s during which time the country was ruled under martial law with then President Ferdinand Marcos. The maturing of civil society under Marcos ironically found its most radical expression in 1986 when these groups initiated a mass uprising (known through- out the world as “People Power”) that overthrew the Marcos administration. The newfound democratic space that succeeded the Marcos years saw wide, ofﬁcial recognition of the role of civil society in public life. In 1987, President Corazon Aquino presided over the drafting of a new Constitution, which sought to institutionalize“People Power” and “encourage non-governmental, community-based, or sectoral organizations that promote the welfare of the nation” (Article II, Section 23, Philippine Constitution of 1987). Speciﬁcally, a new Local Government Code was enacted in 1991 that created Local Development Councils at the local government (or barangay) level and required mem- bership in the councils from civil society representatives from locally accredited organi- zations. These groups, mostly in the form of NGOs and people’s organizations (POs, which are community-based organizations composed of the poor), thus, have a formal role in the everyday business of government. Aside from this formal role in local planning, NGOs and POs also play an important role in some areas of service delivery, such as forest and coastal zone management, low-cost housing, reproductive and family health, and most recently, public procurement and auditing. This occurs in a state that is administratively weak, where governance is often ineffective, and various levels of government fare poorly in the delivery of goods and services. The bureaucracy at all levels suffers from a lack of professionalization, underfunding, and poor management (World Bank 2009). The Local Development Councils are often ineffectual, unable to plan or carry out signiﬁcant development projects, and acquiesce to private interests in public matters. The same is largely true for Congress in which material evidence for success of civil society representation is lacking (Tangkia & Habaradas 2001). The World Bank country assessment report (2009, p. 14) describes governance in the following terms: The inadequate performance of public institutions is caused by: (a) the prevalence of corruption at all levels; (b) weak citizen participation in governance; (c) lack of professionalism and inadequate leadership and management capacities of political leaders; and (d) a bloated and inefﬁcient bureaucracy that results in lack of com- munication and coordination and hampers the delivery of public services. In the face of such a weak state, what is maintained in the Philippines is the unre- mitting power of wealth, as private investment is the primary source of development and livelihood. Thus, a strong type of clientelist politics results from both this and the country’s roots in the landed oligarchy. Agency capture, by both political and economic elites, is a reality in the Philippines, and all levels of government operate (on an informal basis) in a political milieu where the boundaries between public and private are ambigu- ous and porous (Wurfel 1989). Political life in the Philippines is still, despite formal Regulation-by-information E. Lee et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 12 Page 13: progress in developing codes of electoral and administrative procedure, thoroughly patronistic, particularistic, and personalist (Aceron 2009). Citizens learn to seek action on various issues, not by depending on political representatives, but through personal ties and informal routes. The Philippines, then, features a two-sided polity, involving the spectacle of popular democracy and the more latent, informal workings of elite capture. The blurring of public and private domains results in a great degree of discretion, on the part of leaders of local, regional, and national government and bureau chiefs, over the disbursement of public funds. This is exacerbated by the widely recognized practice of using political appointments (of department heads and others) as a way to return favors to family and political allies.5 Civil society in the Philippines is also hampered by a lack of institutionalization into the policymaking process. In a sense, the People Power movement is too deeply ingrained and public interest groups have strategies for protest, but not working engagement with the government. Line agencies routinely do public consultation as a pro forma arrange- ment, often involving an ofﬁcial statement and press release. Yet genuine engagement, by which civil society helps evaluate data, forms policy alternatives, and pressures bureau chiefs, has been lacking. On the other hand, bureaus at all levels (national down to local) are characterized by poor professionalization of the corps as a result of the low salaries – starting at USD 177/month (COA 2010) – and low standards with regard to staff performance. Bureau chiefs respond to personal or private inﬂuence (from the political and economic elites), but there are few mechanisms by which civil society exerts pressure on them. In summary, for complex historical and socio-economic reasons, public administration in the Philip- pines is characterized by weak capacity and the blurring of public and private ends that leads to a dilution of an agency’s mission, to an extent that a primary goal among bureau staff becomes highly political (Monsod 2008). 4.4. Industry dissent and limited administrative capacity LLDA launched PDP without additional resources and adequate training for agency staff. This simply expanded a long list of existing responsibilities and diluted regulatory efforts. Indeed, most countries in the developing world lack the needed resources to implement and sustain new regulations (Farazmand 2009). Although the same problem may also apply to industrialized countries, the scale and scope of the problem in developing countries is incomparable to those in the developed world. Arguably, the lack of material support represents one important reason why public disclosure on a regular basis has not been sustained. Inadequate resources alone cannot fully explain the current practice of PDP, however. Rather, insufﬁcient material resources could be a reason why LLDA attempted to trans- form hierarchical regulation in favor of IBER in the ﬁrst place. We ﬁnd that the agency staff’s incapacity, attributable to the organizational culture and routines, in dealing with unjustiﬁable industry challenges, is largely responsible for the indirect and selective information disclosure to the public. As mentioned earlier, regulatory disclosure in the Philippines is being attempted in an organizational milieu that is characterized by a high level of informality and, at the same time, deliberative practice. Since the People Power Revolution in 1986, followed by the revised Constitution of 1987 guaranteeing democratic governance of the country, admin- Regulation-by-information E. Lee et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 13 Page 14: istrative practice in the Philippines has been marked by a strong emphasis on consulta- tion with relevant stakeholders, sometimes to an extreme degree. In environmental governance, this allowed continuous bargaining between the government and industry actors, occurring in both front- and back-stage of the social discourse (Gilad 2011). In the case of LLDA, limited administrative capacity is inherently linked to the excessive degree of consultation involved in the release of color ratings. The timeline for disclosure is characterized by a long period (more than six months) between the con- struction of color-coded performance ratings and the schedule for their disclosure. This period can be thought of as an unnecessary transaction cost. Although such transactions are an essential element of social practice in the Philippines after democratization, the high degree of transactional practices conﬂicts with the spirit of IBER, which is the free and prompt disclosure of information. When bargaining occurs between data gathering and disclosure, a number of things can happen – for example, manipulation of the information, impedance of the ability of public pressure to act on the polluter, loss of efﬁciency, and the possibility of complete disenfranchisement of the database altogether (such that no data is actually released to the general public). LLDA has neither the material nor managerial wherewithal to negotiate with each ﬁrm prior to the latter’s inclusion in a list of rated polluters, especially if such negotiations take place over extended periods of time. While the statutory language of the Clean Water Act would ostensibly protect the environmental agency from any libel suits, the reality is that LLDA (and DENR, a superior agency) view these suits themselves as a large trans- actional burden, regardless of whether they win cases or not. The administrative burden of litigation is large enough to derail any information disclosure program. Indeed, from the interviews, we conﬁrm that the primary reasons for LLDA’s decision not to disclose the rating results to the public are dissent of industry and threats of legal reprisal against the government agency for libel. A DENR engineer who was involved in the drafting of DAO 2003-26 explained: We cannot disclose the information without approval from the Secretary of DENR. Any disclosure is in his discretion . . . it is a political decision. But it is difﬁcult from the legal perspective. For example, if there is a negative disclosure, these big com- panies may ﬁle a suit against the person who issued it. That’s the main concern we have.
